data.title: Hong Kong stocks waver as oil swings on Middle East tensions
data.description:
Hong Kong stocks swung between gains and losses on Friday as investors traded on headlines about the Middle East tensions and navigated volatile oil prices.
The Hang Seng Index fell 0.1 per cent to 25,471.66 as of 9.46am local time. The Hang Seng Tech Index dropped 0.7 per cent.
On the mainland, the CSI 300 Index climbed 0.9 per cent and the Shanghai Composite Index added 0.3 per cent.
Alibaba Group Holding tumbled 4.1 per cent to HK$126.50 after reporting a 67 per cent slump in quarterly earnings and sluggish revenue growth. Xiaomi sank 6 per cent after launching a new version of its electric vehicle.
Crude oil remained in focus as investors watched fuel prices for clues on whether the US-Israel war on Iran would thrust the world into stagflation or even a recession. Futures contracts on the fuel eased more than 2 per cent on Friday after Israel said it would no longer target energy infrastructure and Iran’s military capability had been degraded. Still, Brent oil traded above US$100 a barrel, a level unsettling investors over higher energy costs.
Other major Asian markets were mixed. South Korea’s Kospi rose 1.1 per cent, while Australia’s S&P/ASX 200 and Taiwan’s Taiex both lost 0.4 per cent. Japan’s market was closed for a holiday.

data.title: Repeated complaints by residents ignored before deadly Tai Po fire, hearing told
data.description:
The blaze was likely to have been caused by cigarettes lighting flammable materials in a light well, Dawes said, citing a preliminary report from a cross-departmental government investigation panel.
He revealed that the Labour Department had inspected the site 16 times in response to smoking complaints, but noted that it never saw workers on scaffolding lighting up and ruled that all the cases “could not be justified”.
That was despite residents repeatedly capturing workers smoking on camera.

data.title: Office vacancy rate in Hong Kong’s Central drops to single digits after 2 years
data.description:
Prime office vacancy rates in Hong Kong’s main business district dropped to single digits for the first time in 26 months, lifting overall rents in the city’s struggling office property market, according to JLL.
The vacancy rate in Central for premium offices fell to 9.9 per cent in February from 10.1 per cent the previous month, the property consultancy said. The last time a single-digit rate was recorded for grade A offices in the district was in December 2023, when it also hit 9.9 per cent, according to JLL’s data.
The citywide prime office vacancy rate also inched down to 13.4 per cent in February from 13.5 per cent a month earlier, with the Wan Chai and Causeway Bay area recording a decrease to 10.2 per cent from 10.3 per cent in its third straight month of falling rates.
Given the improvement, Central’s grade A office rents rose 3.5 per cent in the first two months of the year, after rising 1.2 per cent in January and 2.3 per cent in February, JLL said. This increase supported a 1.1 per cent month-on-month rise in overall office rents in February, the company said.

“With the banking sector remaining the primary driver of leasing activity, and demand focused on new office buildings in core business districts, only two districts have shown early signs of improvement,” said Alex Barnes, managing director of JLL in Hong Kong, Macau and Taiwan.
“This trend is expected to persist throughout the year, while noncore districts such as Kowloon East are likely to remain under pressure,” Barnes said.
CK Asset Holdings, the property developer of the family of billionaire Li Ka-shing, has also expressed optimism about office leasing demand this year.
“Leasing remained under pressure last year, but recent renewals had begun to see low single-digit rental increases,” said Justin Chiu Kwok-hung, executive director at the company, at its results briefing on Thursday afternoon.
With a total investment property portfolio of about 22.4 million sq ft, CK Asset has 3.9 million sq ft of rental office space in Hong Kong, according to its latest annual report.
It reported that it earned HK$1.75 billion (US$223 million) in office rental for all its office assets, including in mainland China, in 2025, lower than the HK$1.78 billion in the previous year.
“We expect rental and sales prices for overall non-residential properties to continue adjusting and to keep seeking support levels,” said David Ma, CEO at Midland Holdings and Midland IC&I. “However, rental and sales prices for core district offices may stabilise first.”
Meanwhile, vacancy rates edged up 0.3 percentage points to 7 per cent in Tsim Sha Tsui and by 0.2 percentage points to 19.4 per cent in Kowloon East, compared with January, JLL said. In Hong Kong East, vacancy was unchanged at 12.8 per cent.
“Vacancy rates across most submarkets, with the exception of Central and Wan Chai-Causeway Bay, remained broadly stable or edged up slightly,” said Cathie Chung, senior director of research at JLL. “Fringe districts such as Kowloon East continued to face downward rental pressure despite already being at record-low rental levels.”
Additional reporting by Peggy Ye

data.title: SFC prepares to inspect Hong Kong IPO listing sponsors amid quality concerns
data.description:
Hong Kong’s market regulator will soon begin inspection of investment banks to assess their roles as sponsors of listing candidates, in its latest effort to uphold the quality of new listings amid a booming market.
The Securities and Futures Commission (SFC) said the inspection would focus on whether banks had complied with its January circular, which outlined expectations on staffing and quality controls when handling initial public offerings (IPOs) in Hong Kong. For example, the SFC expected that one staff member should not oversee more than six deals at once.
“To gatekeep listing applications and further solidify Hong Kong’s position as a trusted fundraising hub, the SFC issued a circular to IPO sponsors in January to raise concerns about deficiencies in listing documents and sponsor misconduct,” the commission said in its quarterly report released on Thursday.
“It is now reviewing the sponsors’ submissions as required by the circular and will commence thematic inspections of sponsors in the near term.”
The move followed findings by the SFC and bourse operator Hong Kong Exchanges and Clearing in the fourth quarter that the quality of sponsors’ work had declined, with some listing application documents deemed substandard.

The SFC told investment banks that it “may take potential regulatory actions to uphold market quality and protect the investing public if substandard behaviours persist or worsen”.
SFC ordered 13 investment banks to review their listing applications and staffing procedures, and to submit reports on improvements, CEO Julia Leung Fung-yee told lawmakers last month. She did not name the firms but said they had collectively handled 433 IPO applications in recent years, accounting for about 70 per cent of new listings in Hong Kong during the period.
“Some sponsors are overly focused on chasing deals and meeting sales targets, without ensuring they have adequate manpower and resources to maintain the quality of their due diligence and paperwork,” she said.
Hong Kong reclaimed the title of the world’s largest IPO market last year, with 114 companies raising US$37.22 billion on the stock exchange’s main board, according to LSEG Data & Analytics. About 500 companies are awaiting listings in Hong Kong, according to exchange data.
“The SFC inspection is a positive move as it will require the investment banks to hire more people to manage the increasing number of new listing applications,” said Tom Chan Pak-lam, honorary president of the Institute of Securities Dealers, an industry body for local stockbrokers. “This will create job opportunities and uphold the quality of the market.”
Chan added that it also “means some investment banks will have to cut down the number of deals they are working on, which may give business opportunities to other sponsors”.

The SFC on Wednesday recommended that the exchange strengthen its regulation of listed companies to better protect investors following a review of 2024 compliance and enforcement cases.
Last week, the SFC joined the Independent Commission Against Corruption (ICAC) in raiding 14 locations, including offices of Guotai Junan International Holdings and Citic Securities’ local unit, in a HK$315 million (US$40 million) insider dealing and corruption probe.
The SFC report also showed the city’s financial sector expanding, with licence applications rising 17 per cent in 2025 to 9,637, including 9,338 individuals and 299 corporations. That brought the total to 3,424 licensed corporations and 47,079 individuals at year-end.
Hong Kong-domiciled funds saw net inflows soar 118.5 per cent year on year to HK$356.7 billion last year, lifting total assets under management 38 per cent to HK$2.28 trillion.
It was a year of “high-quality growth for Hong Kong as a premier international financial centre, as strategic innovation propelled robust capital formation, an accelerating digital ecosystem and a thriving asset management sector”, Leung said in the report.
“Looking ahead, the SFC remains committed to future-proofing Hong Kong’s markets by driving responsible innovation, enhancing resilience and strengthening investor trust.”

data.title: Deadly Tai Po fire revealed ‘unacceptable systemic failings’, inquiry hears
data.description:
Contractors for a renovation project at the site of Hong Kong’s deadliest fire in decades got away with covering up the use of substandard material because of systemic defects in government supervision, a public inquiry into the blaze that claimed 168 lives has heard.
In the first of a series of evidential hearings, a judge-led independent committee was told on Thursday that six “human factors” led to the almost complete failure of fire safety measures at Tai Po’s Wang Fuk Court before seven of its eight residential blocks were engulfed in a 43-hour inferno that broke out on November 26 last year.
The committee’s leading senior counsel, Victor Dawes, noted in his opening speech that the Labour Department, the Fire Services Department and the Housing Bureau’s Independent Checking Unit (ICU) all denied it was their responsibility to ensure the building materials used in the HK$336 million project met required fireproof standards.
When residents’ repeated complaints forced authorities to conduct inspections, the contractor, having been tipped off in advance, partially replaced substandard combustible protective nets used at the site with fire-retardant ones.
“Why would such a major fire happen in Hong Kong, known as an advanced city and for its world-class infrastructure, and why hadn’t the government and contractors been notified of the series of failures and mistakes?” Dawes said.
“This fire has revealed unacceptable systemic failings that cannot be overlooked.”

Among the dead in Hong Kong’s worst fire since 1948 were a firefighter, nine domestic helpers, seven construction workers and a visitor. Nearly 5,000 residents of the devasted estate were displaced.
All eight of Wang Fuk Court’s blocks had been under renovation and covered with bamboo scaffolding and green mesh since the summer of 2024, as concerns remained over possible bid-rigging after the most expensive tender was adopted.
Dawes said the fire started at the light well outside rooms 104 and 105 of Wang Cheong House on November 26, most likely to have been because of a worker smoking nearby.
Surveillance camera footage showed workers pointing fingers at each other after the fire was first discovered at 2.42pm.
Another clip showed a worker smoking on the rooftop of Wang Cheong House at 2.43pm, but Dawes stressed that whether he caused the fire remained uncertain.


Dawes outlined six initially identifiable causes of the disaster, including the use of combustible scaffolding mesh, the fire alarm system’s deactivation, removal of fireproof windows from emergency passages and fire hoses being turned off for months, exceeding the legal limit of 14 days.
Other failures included the use of polyfoam boards to block windows and workers’ smoking habits, an issue that residents had repeatedly raised but was ignored.
The committee heard that the Labour Department inspected the site 16 times over smoking complaints, but ruled that all cases “could not be justified”, although residents repeatedly captured workers smoking on camera.




Responding to some of the complaints, an occupational safety officer from the department explained to residents that “public safety matters” did not fall under its supervision, while noting that relevant construction safety laws did not regulate the fire resistance of scaffolding mesh or other materials.
The department forwarded one complaint to the Fire Services Department, but it also refused to take charge.
“We’re unable to clearly point out which [government] department has ever taken responsibility for the matter,” Dawes said.
Separately, the bureau’s ICU never conducted on-site inspection of Wang Fuk Court’s emergency staircases after the windows were replaced by wooden doors to allow easy access for workers.
The Buildings Department, which was not responsible for overseeing the estate’s renovation work, failed to tell the ICU about its updated internal regulations in 2023, causing the unit to continue to screen written applications for small alteration projects following outdated procedures.
Dawes said different government arms also offered conflicting accounts over the legal requirements of fire-retardant material at construction sites.

While the Buildings Department said the use of polyfoam boards to cover the windows of residents’ homes could be illegal, the ICU contended that they were provisional construction material which was outside the purview of the law.
The Fire Services Department argued that its responsibility was limited to “active fire protection”, including the proper functioning of fire hoses and alarms.
On the issue of scaffolding mesh, Dawes said there was clear evidence that Prestige Construction and Engineering Co, the contractor for the renovations, had instructed scaffolding subcontractor Gain Profit Shed Industry to replace nets damaged in two typhoons last year with ones that were cheaper but not fireproof.
The subcontractor only ordered compliant nets after learning of a pending inspection by the ICU on October 28, replacing the non-compliant portion near the ground.
That was made possible as an ICU inspector had notified project consultant Will Power Architects Company in advance about the date and scope of the check, a move Dawes described as “very concerning”.

Footage of the test that day showed a sample net had burned for 10 seconds, far exceeding the four seconds as purported on a certificate of its fireproof capabilities, but no ICU inspectors expressed surprise at the result.
Dawes said the certificate, dated January 2024 and submitted by the subcontractor to the Labour Department, could have been forged as it bore an outdated name of a mainland Chinese testing authority.
Fourteen members of the housing estate’s former owners’ corporation and seven other residents are among 38 people or organisations named as interested parties, entitling them to make submissions, summon and question witnesses or give evidence at the hearings.
Outside the hearing, a Wang Fuk Court resident who declined to be named said he hoped the probe would hold officials accountable.
Another resident, a man surnamed Kong who lived at Wang Yan House, said the hearing was heart-wrenching and urged the government to take major renovations seriously.
“For our HK$330 million project, is it really something that neighbours can make decisions on? You have so many professional teams – you need to be commissioned, licensed and qualified before you can be a consultant or a contractor,” he said.
“So there is no reason you would subcontract it back to us residents who are completely unfamiliar with this field. We aren’t doing this full-time at the owners’ corporation. Even full-timers can’t handle it.”
Dawes will continue his opening speech at City Gallery in Central on Friday, with the first witness expected to be called next week.

data.title: Why the slow progress for Hong Kong’s university town?
data.description:
Hong Kong’s Baptist University placed a big bet when it told the government in 2023 that it was interested in moving its campus from the upmarket Kowloon Tong residential area to a proposed new academic town near the mainland Chinese border.
The 70-year-old university’s move to the Northern Metropolis megaproject will mean giving up a prime site for a spot where most of the land has yet to be prepared for construction, although its proximity to mainland tech hub Shenzhen and the Lok Ma Chau Loop, partly earmarked for an innovation and technology park, has obvious appeal.
“The Northern Metropolis neighbours the most innovative city in mainland China, and while Hong Kong is developing innovation and technology, we can utilise the resources in the vicinity,” university president Alexander Wai Ping-kong told the South China Morning Post in an interview this month.
“Our students and professors can reach Shenzhen and the Lok Ma Chau Loop directly and conveniently.”
The university town is one of the core developments in the megaproject that aims to turn 30,000 hectares (74,132 acres) of land near the border into an engine for economic growth and a housing hub.
Baptist University is not the only one that has ambitions, with 18 other local public and private institutions also submitting proposals to the government in 2023 to set up operations or build facilities there.
Some have proposed setting up satellite campuses to provide more academic and research space for developing innovative programmes, while others plan to promote vocational education involving cooperation with industries and integration of science and education.
A number of them want to set up shared facilities, such as animal centres, data centres, student hostels, sports facilities and libraries.
Nearly three years on, the higher education sector is still waiting for details about the 100-hectare academic hub, prompting some university leaders to urge faster planning amid uncertainties over land, while noting that the development is not without challenges, especially as geopolitical tensions escalate.

What’s the plan?
The Northern Metropolis University Town proposed by Chief Executive John Lee Ka-chiu in 2023 aims to allow local tertiary institutions to collaborate with renowned mainland and overseas counterparts, creating synergy by sharing resources and enhancing partnerships with industry.
The government is set to unveil a “development conceptual framework” for the university town in the first half of this year, with the overall approach and position to be based on industry-led principles and national development strategies. The town is expected to operate on a self-financing basis.
The proposed project has grown in size from 60 hectares in 2023 to the current 100 hectares, occupying 0.3 per cent of the whole metropolis.
The expansion was prompted by calls from the community, the establishment of Hong Kong’s third medical school and the goal of turning the city into an international higher education hub, the government said.
A year after the idea of a university town was floated, the government also suggested creating a “Study in Hong Kong” brand to attract more overseas students through the provision of scholarships and other incentives. The admissions quota for non-local students will be raised further to 50 per cent of the number of local students in the new academic year.




The university town will be spread over three sites, covering about nine hectares in Hung Shui Kiu and Ha Tsuen, about 52 hectares in Ngau Tam Mei and about 40 hectares in the New Territories North, which will be ready for use in 2026, 2028 and 2030, respectively. They are equivalent in size to about 13, 73 and 56 football pitches, respectively.
Hung Shui Kiu is aimed at developing professional services and modern logistics, Ngau Tam Mei will be positioned as an innovative technology zone, while the New Territories North site will be turned into a commerce and industry area.
The first batch of land for the town comprises three sites in Hung Shui Kiu and will be rolled out shortly for application by public universities and universities of applied sciences for campus development, with the authorities earmarking HK$10 billion (US$1.28 billion) for interest-bearing loans.
Apart from tertiary institutions, the government has said it will reserve sites in the Northern Metropolis for international schools, but it is unclear where they will be sited.
All about land
Wai of Baptist University described the progress of the university town as “too slow”, urging authorities to expedite comprehensive planning and clarify their positioning, as continued uncertainty made it difficult to engage potential mainland and overseas partners.
“I need something concrete before holding discussions [with other institutions],” Wai said.
“Once I have negotiated with them, especially mainland universities, they will expect everything to progress quickly: funds will be secured, land will be allocated, and then they can come soon.”
Wai said the planned scale of the town remained relatively limited compared with mainland counterparts.
“The key point is that the 100 hectares is to be shared by the universities; it is not a vast space,” Wai noted.
The University of Hong Kong (HKU), the city’s oldest university, plans to build an international campus there to foster more global collaborations.
Cherry Tse Ling Kit-ching, HKU’s executive vice-president for administration and finance, said land was the starting point for all calculations.
“We do not know how big the land is and how many buildings I can build there. It is even difficult for me to talk about whether I will borrow money from the government. The first thing is not about money but land. If the government does not give us land, nothing can be discussed,” said Tse, a former permanent secretary for education.
“We will try to get every piece of land in the university town. Without land, we do not know how much we should put in, how to design the buildings and so on.”
She said HKU would consider issuing bonds and fundraising apart from the option of taking on an interest-bearing loan from the government.

Professor John Lee Chi-kin, president of the Education University of Hong Kong, echoed calls for detailed planning to be accelerated to provide institutions with greater certainty.
“What are the priorities of the university town? We wish to know sooner,” he said. “If some institutions realise they are likely to stand little chance, can we help them explore alternative options?”
Lee urged the government to consider rezoning other suitable sites in the Northern Metropolis for educational use, thereby easing universities’ efforts to establish footholds in the area.
“A university town involves planning for transport, community and populations,” he said. “Simply rezoning a site for educational use will be more flexible and will not require that much time.”
Professor Teng Jin-guang, president of Polytechnic University (PolyU), noted that the Northern Metropolis was a complicated project, but institutions needed land to plan ahead, particularly when the related process took time.
“The development of the Northern Metropolis is highly complex. We cannot say it is slow. We hope the pace can accelerate from here, because the country’s overall development is progressing very quickly,” Teng said.
“There is no such thing as an ideal or non-ideal timeline. We need land right now. There is a process involved, but we hope to progress as fast as possible.”
Until now, no single plot of land has been formally allocated to any institution except for the third medical school to be operated by Hong Kong University of Science and Technology in Ngau Tam Mei, and to the Hong Kong Academy for Performing Arts.
The latter will be given a site for a campus in the New Territories North New Town.
Aside from concerns over land, escalating geopolitical tensions could also pose difficulties for universities in securing overseas collaboration, Wai said.
He cited a futile attempt by Baptist University to partner with a private American medical university while bidding to operate Hong Kong’s third medical school.
“I had very good discussions with professors and the faculty,” Wai said. “But at the vice-chancellor level, they told me there was a problem, and legal issues came up.”
The American university subsequently withdrew from talks, citing directives from the state government, according to Wai.
He said the experience also showed how difficult it could be to attract foreign academics.
“When it comes to attracting talent – especially in the technology field – ethnic Chinese candidates are generally not an issue. To lure non-ethnic Chinese ones is difficult,” Wai said.
“I have invited some foreign professors, and collaboration and co-publishing papers are not a problem. But when it involves formally bringing them to Hong Kong, even part time, they hesitate and worry.”

The government’s thinking
Secretary for Education Christine Choi Yuk-lin told the SCMP last August that authorities hoped to make the best use of land – a valuable resource in Hong Kong – in the university town, adding that officials would visit educational hubs elsewhere for reference.
“Hong Kong’s university town might be the smallest in the world,” Choi said.
She said authorities were still assessing the institutions’ proposals to see if they added value and were innovative, stressing that simply moving a campus or faculties was not the objective.
“If it’s just about moving to a new location, there’s no added value. We need to see if the proposal brings new elements to Hong Kong’s higher education system,” she said.
She revealed authorities had already met some presidents of renowned institutions outside the city who had visited Hong Kong to see whether they would launch a branch.
“But it is still in the initial stage because the development of the town by institutions is on a self-financing basis; they have to bring capital and their talent to Hong Kong. They also have to discuss how much they can afford after visiting Hong Kong … a self-financing mode is actually a challenge,” she said.
Last June, senior staff from the International Institute for Management Development (IMD) in Switzerland met officials from the Northern Metropolis Coordination Office to learn about the megaproject and the university town.
The office said it “looks forward to further exploring cooperation opportunities with IMD”.
In December, Peking University’s party secretary, He Guangcai, met city leader John Lee Ka-chiu in Beijing.
Lee said after the meeting the university indicated that it intended to set up a research institute in the university town in the future.

Overseas lessons
Victor Kwok Hoi-kit, a deputy research director at Our Hong Kong Foundation think tank, said the planning for the university town could take reference from overseas examples such as Incheon Global Campus in South Korea and Education City in Doha, Qatar.
These education hubs had successfully attracted overseas universities and allowed institutions to share some facilities and cooperate with each other while interacting with local industries to drive economic growth, he said.
The 1,200-hectare Education City in Doha hosts campuses of seven foreign universities, including Carnegie Mellon University, Georgetown University, Northwestern University and HEC Paris, as well as one local institution, Hamad Bin Khalifa University.
The South Korean campus covers about 30 hectares and plans to expand by another 11 hectares.
Three American universities, the State University of New York, George Mason University and the University of Utah, along with Ghent University from Belgium, offer degree programmes there. It also hosts a smart city research centre by Stanford University.
Located next to Incheon Free Economic Zone near the capital Seoul, the institutions in the area have also fostered cooperation with leading biotech companies such as Samsung Biologics and Celltrion, providing their graduates with a better chance of landing jobs in the industry.
Kwok said both projects had successfully managed to share resources among different institutions, making them useful examples for Hong Kong to learn from.
He added that Hong Kong’s universities should not regard the project simply as an expansion of their infrastructure.
“It would be a waste if no global institutions are involved, and it would also be a waste if there was no meaningful interaction between teaching, research and industry,” he said.
“The government should think comprehensively when laying out the plan for the university town, and it should be equipped with business-related support.
“For example, there should be some accelerator or incubator programmes to help students turn their intellectual property into products or companies, so as to nurture Hong Kong’s own unicorns.”
Growing geopolitical tensions would inevitably pose difficulties in luring top-tier global institutions, but authorities should still try to reach out and prioritise those with a good record of integrating research with industry, Kwok said.
“For these universities, establishing their presence in Hong Kong gives them access to resources in mainland China and is a good opportunity for cultural exchange,” he said.

data.title: Huawei capitalises on OpenClaw frenzy to boost computing demand for its chips
data.description:
Chinese telecommunications gear giant Huawei Technologies is capitalising on the OpenClaw frenzy with new enterprise agent tools and a series of Kunpeng and Ascend compute offerings, as it bets on surging demand for agentic artificial intelligence services.
The Shenzhen-based company announced at its China Partner Conference on Thursday that it would launch AgentArts, an agent development platform for enterprises, on April 30 for public beta testing, followed by the official release of the open-source version on May 30.
The platform was designed to improve the efficiency of AI agent development, cutting delivery time by more than 60 per cent, according to the company.
“AI agents have moved quickly from concept to large-scale deployment, and OpenClaw, in particular, has become a global phenomenon in the past month or so, with its extremely strong capabilities of autonomous execution,” said Wang Tao, executive director at Huawei, at the conference.

The expanding deployment of agentic AI across industries had driven exponential growth in token consumption, and Huawei aimed to meet the rising demand for computing power with its Kunpeng and Ascend platforms, Wang said.
“In 2026, we will open our hardware series, including modules, standard cards, servers and supernodes, to our partners, and build the foundation of computing power together,” he added.
Huawei has been challenging global rivals, such as Nvidia, with its self-developed Ascend specialised AI computing platform and Kunpeng general-purpose computing foundation, positioning itself as an alternative to US-led AI systems.
The rapid advancement of agentic AI in 2026 has placed central processing units (CPUs) in an essential role of managing agent tools, pre-processes data and coordinates the entire system, said Ma Haixu, a vice-president at Huawei.
“The surge in AI computing is giving a strong lift to general-purpose computing, which brings unprecedented development opportunities for the Kunpeng ecosystem,” Ma said.
Huawei is aiming to capture the shift with its Kunpeng 920C, 950 and the next-gen 960 CPU series, alongside its Taishan supernode clusters, as the hardware foundation for agentic AI, Ma said.
The growing demand for computing services and infrastructure has also become a driving force for Huawei’s enterprise-facing business.
Huawei’s revenue from enterprise and government business in China crossed 100 billion yuan (US$14.6 billion) in 2025 for the first time, Wang said.
The company had also expanded its eight “legions” – cross-departmental teams dedicated to specific industries – to 48 groups to better understand the needs of each industry, according to Li Peng, president of the China region at Huawei.
Huawei posted revenue of more than 880 billion yuan in 2025, the second highest on record for the US-sanctioned company. Its highest ever was 891 billion yuan in 2020, which came after the first US sanctions on its lucrative mobile phone and international operations.
The company has been accelerating its comeback over the past few years, achieving breakthroughs, including in its Ascend AI chips, without relying on American technology.
In September, Huawei unveiled plans to launch upgraded Ascend AI chips over the next three years.

data.title: Hong Kong finance chief stresses flexibility in pitch for Northern Metropolis
data.description:
Hong Kong is ready to discuss land, tax incentives and financial support for businesses setting up in the Northern Metropolis, the finance chief has told a trade seminar in Beijing, stressing a “flexible and innovative” approach will be taken with developing the megaproject.
Financial Secretary Paul Chan Mo-po on Thursday also met Xia Baolong, Beijing’s point man on Hong Kong affairs, who underscored the central government’s support for the city’s efforts to bolster its competitiveness through its first local five-year plan.
Delivering the keynote address at the Hong Kong Trade Development Council seminar, Chan stressed the Northern Metropolis’s position as a cooperation platform in the Greater Bay Area, noting the cross-border innovation and technology (I&T) cooperation zone in Hetao would promote the flow of personnel, resources, capital and data across the border with mainland China.
“We welcome mainland and overseas enterprises to actively participate in the Northern Metropolis, bringing cutting-edge technologies and talent, as well as upstream and downstream industry chains,” he said.
“With Hong Kong’s strengths, we can explore international markets and support Hong Kong’s development as an international I&T hub.
“The administration is ready to offer its preferential policy packages, while we will adopt a flexible and innovative cooperation model. We can talk about land, tax incentives, financial support and co-investment.”

Chan’s appeal came two days after the administration proposed a legal framework for a dedicated law to accelerate the megaproject’s development, which included a proposal to shorten land approvals from nine to two months.
The Northern Metropolis involves turning 30,000 hectares (74,130 acres) of land near the city’s border into an economic powerhouse and housing hub.
The minister told the seminar that I&T development required support from enterprises and industries, noting the city had strong abilities in research, an increasingly robust I&T ecosystem and a pool of mainland and overseas talent and resources.
Hong Kong also had advantages in developing artificial intelligence, new materials and new energy, as well as technology related to life sciences and finance, he said.
Speaking at the same event, Yuan Xiaoming, China’s assistant minister of commerce, said mainland authorities would foster closer economic and trade arrangements with the Hong Kong government.
He Yaqiong, director general of the department of consumer products industry at the Ministry of Industry and Information Technology, said the agency would focus on helping the city integrate into national industrial modernisation efforts and help cross-border I&T cooperation.
Earlier on Thursday, Chan met Xia, director of the Hong Kong and Macau Affairs Office, and briefed him about the city’s overall economic and financial situations.
According to a government statement, Xia underscored Beijing’s support for the city’s efforts to enhance competitiveness and expressed confidence the local administration would lead all sectors of society and be practical in devising Hong Kong’s five-year plan.

The blueprint would inject greater impetus into the local economy, as it would further consolidate Hong Kong’s role as an international centre for finance, shipping and trade, as well as expedite the city’s development as an international global I&T hub, he said.
Separately in Hong Kong, Legislative Council president Starry Lee Wai-king said she had briefed fellow lawmakers about a collaborative mechanism between the legislature and the government to gather and analyse public opinion.
Lee said lawmakers would form several working groups and complete its work within three to four months to assist the government in preparing the five-year plan.
Chief Executive John Lee Ka-chiu announced the set-up of the mechanism on Tuesday, after Chinese Vice-Premier Ding Xuexiang earlier stressed the importance of an executive-led system and called on Legco to deliver more practical results.

data.title: Firefighter killed in Tai Po blaze may have entered wrong building, inquiry told
data.description:
A firefighter killed in Hong Kong’s deadliest blaze in decades may have entered the wrong building amid the initial chaos at the scene and fallen from a height, the first day of a public hearing into the inferno has heard.
More details surrounding the death of 37-year-old Ho Wai-ho, among the first batch of firefighters sent to tackle the blaze, emerged as the independent committee set up by the government to investigate the fire at the Wang Fuk Court residential complex in Tai Po held its first evidential session on Thursday.
Victor Dawes SC, the lead barrister for the committee, said in his opening statement that when Ho’s body was found on open ground, his breathing apparatus was not with him and some of his gear was recovered on the 31st floor of Wang Tai House, one of seven residential blocks engulfed by the blaze.
“It is likely that, facing an extremely adverse situation on the 31st floor of Wang Tai House, he removed the breathing apparatus, broke the windows and tried to escape from there. But he eventually fell from height,” Dawes said.
“A preliminary autopsy showed that Ho had breathed in excessive carbon monoxide before his death, with scratches, burns and broken bones found [on his body].”
The inferno broke out on November 26, killing at least 168 people, including Ho, and displacing nearly 5,000 residents.

Surveillance camera footage played at the hearing showed Ho exiting a lift on the 25th floor of Wang Tai House at about 3.15pm. He sent a “mayday” signal seven minutes later.
Dawes said Ho had been called to a rescue on the 27th floor of Wang Cheong House, but might have gone to the wrong building amid the chaotic situation.
“He could not tell which building he was in, and this might have delayed the endeavour to rescue him,” Dawes said.
Ho was eventually found on open ground outside Wang Tai House’s lobby and rushed to Prince of Wales Hospital in Sha Tin, where he was pronounced dead.
Ho had served in the Fire Services Department for nine years and was stationed at Sha Tin Fire Station at the time of the incident.
He was laid to rest in December at Gallant Garden in Wo Hop Shek Cemetery, where civil servants who die in the line of duty for Hong Kong are buried.
The committee, chaired by Justice David Luk Kai-hong, was established to investigate the fire’s cause and identify any systemic issues surrounding large-scale maintenance projects in Hong Kong.
It is expected to deliver a report to the government within nine months of starting work in late December.
The chaos was underscored by a similar mix-up during the early stages of the fire – which raged for more than 43 hours – at the 999 emergency call centre.
A caller, who lived in flat 2307 of Wang Tai House, called 999 at 3.19pm, about half an hour after the initial flames were spotted.
The responder transferred the call to fire services but incorrectly stated that the caller lived in Wang Cheong House, prompting him to clarify the address. It also took about half a minute for the call to be connected to the department’s line.
Dawes said the longest waiting time for an emergency call to be answered reached four minutes, and he urged the committee to examine whether there was room to improve the line’s performance.

data.title: CK Hutchison vows to pursue  ‘all legal remedies’ over seized Panama ports
data.description:
Hong Kong conglomerate CK Hutchison has said it will pursue “all available legal remedies” through both national and international proceedings to resolve a dispute over its two Panama Canal ports which were seized by authorities in February.
Amid the widening conflict in the Middle East which could further complicate its multibillion-dollar global ports sale, the ports-to-telecoms group downplayed the possible impact of a Strait of Hormuz blockade on its business, saying its diversified network could even benefit from higher storage revenue, as shipping diversions boosted inventory demand.
At a results briefing on Thursday, CK Hutchison’s management was asked to provide an update on the proposed sale of more than 40 ports worldwide to a consortium led by BlackRock and Mediterranean Shipping Company (MSC).
Group co-managing director Frank John Sixt said both the conglomerate and its subsidiary Panama Ports Company (PPC) continued to “work vigorously” with their legal advisers and would “provide updates when that is appropriate”.
“We are pursuing all available legal remedies through both national and international proceedings,” he said. “We are in discussions with the original consortium members as well as a significant financial investor and a major strategic investor from China.”
The group reaffirmed its commitment to resolving the dispute in a way that was fair and protected the interests of shareholders.
Caught in the cross-currents of Sino-US tensions, CK Hutchison is navigating a delayed US$23 billion deal to sell 43 global port assets – including the strategic Panama Canal container terminals at Balboa and Cristobal – to a BlackRock-led consortium.
Initially announced in early 2025, the sale has faced regulatory roadblocks from Beijing and a subsequent annulment of port concession contracts granted by the Panama government.
Reports suggest that regulators in mainland China want a larger role for state-owned Cosco in any arrangement involving the canal terminals.
When asked on Wednesday about Cosco’s potential involvement in the sale, Zhu Tao, executive director and chairman, declined to comment.
At the results briefing, group co-managing director Dominic Lai Kai-ming addressed concerns over potential disruptions from the conflict in the Middle East, with Iran having virtually blocked the Strait of Hormuz – a key global oil route – since the United States and Israel attacked it on February 28.
“Our ports network is very extensive, so a single event has limited impact on the overall business,” he said. “In the long term, if there is a shipping disruption, it often drives up storage demand as inventory builds up, so we actually see quite good increases in that area.”
Its diversified footprint – 53 ports across 24 countries with 295 berths – and 3 per cent throughput growth to 90.1 million twenty-foot equivalent units in 2025 help cushion shocks.
The group said it anticipates slower global trade growth this year amid geopolitical risks and China-US tensions. It noted that while a prolonged conflict could divert shipping, the overall impact should be limited, as other ports in its network were expected to benefit from the diversions.

Chairman Victor Li Tzar-kuo said geopolitical pressures had complicated ongoing discussions regarding new arrangements for the disposal of interests in the group’s global port operations outside Panama, Hong Kong and the mainland.
He emphasised the group’s resilience. “As a multinational enterprise with diversified businesses around the world, risk management is relatively easy to control,” he said.
“Macro challenges may affect some businesses, but others will see good growth – for example, rising oil prices make Cenovus Energy one of the group’s highlights, with daily production approaching one million barrels after last year’s MEG acquisition, higher than some countries,” he said, referring to the group’s investment in the Canadian oil and gas producer.
Li added that the group’s finances were at their most robust level, positioning it to manage challenges and seize opportunities amid complex global politics and technological shifts.
“We will continue to invest in countries that respect contracts and have sound legal protections for investors, and in projects with long-term stable cash flows. If there are good projects that meet our internal rate of return, we will definitely be interested,” he said.
CK Hutchison’s ports and related services division posted revenue of nearly HK$49 billion (US$6.25 billion) in 2025, up 8 per cent from 2024.
Panamanian authorities wrested control of the Balboa and Cristobal ports in February following a ruling by the country’s top court that declared the concession held by PPC unconstitutional.
PPC has accused Panama of failing to respond to its arbitration request by the March 13 deadline and is seeking at least US$2 billion in compensation.
The group is pursuing all available remedies to resolve the dispute, including international arbitration in New York initiated by PPC under the Balboa and Cristobal agreements, plus a separate claim under the UK-Panama bilateral investment treaty.

data.title: Hong Kong customs arrests 5 people, seizes HK$2.2 million in counterfeit eyewear
data.description:
Hong Kong customs officers have arrested five people and seized 2,500 pairs of suspected counterfeit eyewear worth about HK$2.2 million (US$280,680) in a citywide operation.
Officers from the Customs and Excise Department carried out the special enforcement action on Tuesday and Wednesday, targeting the sale of counterfeit goods.
Officers raided five retail shops in Cheung Sha Wan, Kwai Shing, Tsuen Wan and Fanling, as well as a supplier in Kwun Tong, seizing the items with the assistance of trademark owners.
Three directors and two staff members, aged 42 to 58, were arrested.
The investigation was ongoing and further arrests had not been ruled out, a spokesman said.
Customs said the counterfeit sunglasses were typically priced 20 to 30 per cent below market value and lacked original packaging and accessories.
The department said it would continue to step up inspections and enforcement to combat the sale of counterfeit goods. Consumers were reminded to buy from reputable shops and verify the goods’ authenticity with trademark owners or authorised agents if in doubt.
Traders were also warned that selling counterfeit goods was a serious crime.
Under the Trade Descriptions Ordinance, anyone who sells or possesses for sale goods with a forged trademark commits an offence. The maximum penalty is a fine of HK$500,000 and five years’ imprisonment.

data.title: Paralegals among 16 people arrested over ‘crash-for-cash’ scams in Hong Kong
data.description:
Hong Kong police have arrested 16 more people, including paralegals, over traffic accident compensation claims worth more than HK$3.6 million (US$459,290) tied to a wave of “crash-for-cash” scams.
The suspects, 11 men and five women aged 28 to 65, included three paralegals at a law firm in Mong Kok that was raided last month, the force said on Thursday.
The other suspects were the claimants and worked in a variety of jobs, including IT technician, beautician, logistics worker, tutor, property management worker and electrician.
The 16 suspects were arrested over 11 insurance claims totalling HK$3.6 million, all originating from the same law firm, according to police.
In one case, an IT technician claimed about a month of sick leave after a traffic accident that did not involve any collision.
“Investigations found that the person continued to go to work during the sick leave period, receiving HK$60,000 in salary, but he had claimed to an insurance company that he had lost over HK$100,000 of income,” Superintendent Charles Fung Pui-kei of the commercial crime bureau said.
Police were last month investigating more than 100 cases tied to crash-for-cash scams, in which claimants filed massive insurance claims or civil claims long after authorities had closed minor traffic cases. Some claims listed accidents for which no record existed at all.
The latest investigation revealed four tactics used to submit the fraudulent claims, according to police.
The perpetrators fabricated income proof to increase the amount of compensation and made false claims about lost income during prolonged sick leave.
They also exaggerated the severity of injuries with falsified doctor’s notes and submitted fake purchase receipts for claims.
Fung said some claimants were out of the city on the dates shown on their doctor’s certificate, leading to suspicions they had conspired with some general practitioners to produce the fake documents.
Police have so far received 430 reports about suspected crash-for-cash scams, worth HK$106 million in claims, with more than 280 cases concerning the same law firm.
In an operation earlier this month, 10 boxes of documents were seized from the law firm.
The firm’s owner later filed an application for a judicial review over the professional privilege of the seized documents, saying their content should not be revealed without a court order.
“We are handling matters of legal privilege concerning [the documents] with the Department of Justice,” acting chief superintendent Fanny Kung Hing-fun said. “We are also continuing our investigation of the case, examining files and claim documents.”
Using fraudulent means to change the amount of compensation claims, or conspiring with others to do so, could constitute an offence, she warned.
The maximum punishment for fraud or conspiracy to defraud is 14 years in prison.
The force previously arrested two doctors and a married couple for allegedly masterminding more than 20 scams last month, before searching a law firm in Mong Kok a few days later.

data.title: Recession fears grip markets as energy facilities under fire in Middle East
data.description:
A recession mindset took hold among global investors after strikes on energy infrastructure in the Middle East heightened fears that disruption to production and distribution would keep oil prices elevated for longer, darkening the global economic outlook.
Crude prices staged a comeback on Thursday after Iran attacked a liquefied petroleum gas site in Qatar in retaliation for Israel’s strikes on its South Pars gas field. Brent futures surged 5.5 per cent to US$113.53 a barrel in London, and contracts for West Texas Intermediate jumped 1 per cent to US$97.44.
“The attack on Qatari gas fields has taken the Gulf crisis to a more dangerous level for the global economy,” said Gary Dugan, CEO of The Global CIO Office, which advises family offices and ultra-high-net-worth investors. “Disruption to both oil and gas supplies is pushing prices higher and raises the risk of genuine fuel shortages that could materially crimp global growth.”
The outlook triggered a broad risk-off mode among investors, as the narrative following the closure of the Strait of Hormuz quickly shifted from energy shortage to supply disruption.
The US dollar index held up firmly at around 100, indicating investors were looking for safe havens, while stock markets saw sell-offs. After the S&P 500 sank 1.4 per cent overnight, the Hang Seng Index slid 2 per cent on Thursday, while the Shanghai Composite Index of yuan-denominated stocks closed 1.4 per cent lower after briefly dropping below the 4,000-point threshold.
Meanwhile, the yield on longer-dated US Treasuries edged higher, reflecting fears of resurgent inflation. Gold also retreated, with investors taking profits to cover margin calls linked to other asset classes.
Investors had barely priced in the threat of global recession before the escalation. Risk assets had remained relatively resilient on expectations that the US-Israel assault against Iran would end quickly. Even after Iran’s blockage of the Strait of Hormuz – a marine corridor responsible for about a fifth of the world’s oil flows – investors stayed upbeat that it was simply a logistics snarl and would not evolve into a true energy crisis.
The attack on infrastructure facilities changed that mindset, forcing investors to take risks off the table as the challenge changed from re-routing ships to repairing physical damage, which could take weeks or even months, according to Stephen Innes, a managing partner at SPI Asset Management.
“The moment upstream Iranian energy assets were hit, the entire pricing framework flipped from logistics to geology,” he said. “You can hedge a tanker delay, but you cannot hedge a wounded gas or oilfield. Tankers can be re-routed, and pipelines can become larger conduits for flow, but damaged production capacity has to be rebuilt, not redirected.”

With oil trading above US$100 a barrel, global growth could be shaved by 0.4 percentage points while inflation could surge by 0.7 percentage points, according to economists.
The recession risk also increased after Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell signalled that a prolonged war would add more uncertainty to the outlook for monetary policies. Rates traders dialled back bets on the number of interest-rate reductions expected this year – to one from two – after the Fed stood pat on borrowing costs at the year’s second policy meeting.
“Should the significant risk that the conflict drags on materialise, and oil remain well above US$100 per barrel for longer, the inflationary effects will feed through into a wider basket of goods, and we could be looking at the next Fed move being a hike later this year, not a cut,” said Jon Butcher, an economist at Aberdeen Investments.

data.title: Alibaba discloses GPU output for first time as quarterly profit misses estimates
data.description:
Alibaba Group Holding, China’s e-commerce and artificial intelligence powerhouse, on Thursday for the first time disclosed the production progress of its T-Head chip unit, as quarterly results missed estimates.
It reported a 2 per cent year-on-year increase in revenue to 284.8 billion yuan (US$40.7 billion) for the December quarter, but missed the 289.8 billion yuan consensus forecast compiled by Bloomberg.
The company also “brought its proprietary GPU [graphics processing unit] into production at scale”. The unit, mentioned for the first time in Alibaba’s earnings, delivered more than 100,000 units of its most advanced AI chip, the Zhenwu 810E, the South China Morning Post reported in January.
The results come as the group’s core e-commerce business remains constrained by weak consumer spending amid macro headwinds, while its cloud and AI operations continue to gain traction but weigh on near-term profitability.
Net profit attributable to ordinary shareholders dropped 67 per cent to 16.3 billion yuan for the third quarter.

Alibaba’s adjusted Ebita – earnings before interest, taxes and amortisation, an alternative measure of a company’s financial health – fell 57 per cent year on year to 23.4 billion yuan, missing analysts’ estimate of 31.4 billion yuan.
Alibaba’s push into instant retail – an area of heavy investment over the past year – continued to drive growth in the December quarter, with revenue rising 56 per cent to 20.8 billion yuan. The company said it was now prioritising improvements in unit economics after an initial phase of aggressive subsidies to build scale.
“This quarter, Alibaba maintained strong investments across our core pillars of AI and consumption,” said CEO Eddie Wu Yongming, adding that “AI is and will continue to be one of our primary growth engines”.
Alibaba, owner of the South China Morning Post, has positioned AI and cloud computing as its primary growth engines, touting its “full-stack” capabilities spanning infrastructure, foundation models, cloud services and applications amid a global surge in AI investment and adoption.
Revenue in its Cloud Intelligence Group rose 36 per cent to 43.3 billion yuan, marking a fifth consecutive quarter of double-digit growth. Revenue from AI-related products maintained triple-digit expansion.
Capital expenditure in the reported quarter reached 29 billion yuan, down from 31.7 billion yuan a year earlier.
Consumer-facing Qwen, powered by its namesake foundation models, surpassed 300 million monthly active users across all platforms at the end of February, the company said, adding that it had accelerated integrating use cases from its consumer ecosystem into the AI app.
Alibaba has accelerated organisational changes over the past year to keep pace with the fast-evolving AI landscape. Earlier this week, it established a new Alibaba Token Hub business group to centralise core AI operations around the creation, distribution and application of “tokens” – the basic units of AI computing – across most of its AI product stack.
E-commerce, Alibaba’s main cash cow, saw revenue rise 6 per cent to 159.4 billion yuan, with adjusted Ebita of 34.6 billion yuan.
The group’s domestic e-commerce business generated 131.6 billion yuan, better than 102.9 billion yuan in the September quarter, buoyed by the year-end seasonal peak, while customer management revenue increased 1 per cent to 102.7 billion yuan.
Revenue from the international digital commerce group rose 3 per cent to 32.4 billion yuan, while adjusted Ebita recorded a loss of 2 billion yuan.
Revenue from “all other” segments – including its maps service, supermarket, sports and entertainment units – stood at 67.3 billion yuan in the reported quarter, a decrease of 25 per cent year on year.

data.title: SFC prepares to inspect Hong Kong IPO listing sponsors amid quality concerns
data.description:
Hong Kong’s market regulator will soon begin inspection of investment banks to assess their roles as sponsors of listing candidates, in its latest effort to uphold the quality of new listings amid a booming market.
The Securities and Futures Commission (SFC) said the inspection would focus on whether banks had complied with its January circular, which outlined expectations on staffing and quality controls when handling initial public offerings (IPOs) in Hong Kong. For example, the SFC expected that one staff member should not oversee more than six deals at once.
“To gatekeep listing applications and further solidify Hong Kong’s position as a trusted fundraising hub, the SFC issued a circular to IPO sponsors in January to raise concerns about deficiencies in listing documents and sponsor misconduct,” the commission said in its quarterly report released on Thursday.
“It is now reviewing the sponsors’ submissions as required by the circular and will commence thematic inspections of sponsors in the near term.”
The move followed findings by the SFC and bourse operator Hong Kong Exchanges and Clearing in the fourth quarter that the quality of sponsors’ work had declined, with some listing application documents deemed substandard.

The SFC told investment banks that it “may take potential regulatory actions to uphold market quality and protect the investing public if substandard behaviours persist or worsen”.
SFC ordered 13 investment banks to review their listing applications and staffing procedures, and to submit reports on improvements, CEO Julia Leung Fung-yee told lawmakers last month. She did not name the firms but said they had collectively handled 433 IPO applications in recent years, accounting for about 70 per cent of new listings in Hong Kong during the period.
“Some sponsors are overly focused on chasing deals and meeting sales targets, without ensuring they have adequate manpower and resources to maintain the quality of their due diligence and paperwork,” she said.
Hong Kong reclaimed the title of the world’s largest IPO market last year, with 114 companies raising US$37.22 billion on the stock exchange’s main board, according to LSEG Data & Analytics. About 500 companies are awaiting listings in Hong Kong, according to exchange data.
“The SFC inspection is a positive move as it will require the investment banks to hire more people to manage the increasing number of new listing applications,” said Tom Chan Pak-lam, honorary president of the Institute of Securities Dealers, an industry body for local stockbrokers. “This will create job opportunities and uphold the quality of the market.”
Chan added that it also “means some investment banks will have to cut down the number of deals they are working on, which may give business opportunities to other sponsors”.

The SFC on Wednesday recommended that the exchange strengthen its regulation of listed companies to better protect investors following a review of 2024 compliance and enforcement cases.
Last week, the SFC joined the Independent Commission Against Corruption (ICAC) in raiding 14 locations, including offices of Guotai Junan International Holdings and Citic Securities’ local unit, in a HK$315 million (US$40 million) insider dealing and corruption probe.
The SFC report also showed the city’s financial sector expanding, with licence applications rising 17 per cent in 2025 to 9,637, including 9,338 individuals and 299 corporations. That brought the total to 3,424 licensed corporations and 47,079 individuals at year-end.
Hong Kong-domiciled funds saw net inflows soar 118.5 per cent year on year to HK$356.7 billion last year, lifting total assets under management 38 per cent to HK$2.28 trillion.
It was a year of “high-quality growth for Hong Kong as a premier international financial centre, as strategic innovation propelled robust capital formation, an accelerating digital ecosystem and a thriving asset management sector”, Leung said in the report.
“Looking ahead, the SFC remains committed to future-proofing Hong Kong’s markets by driving responsible innovation, enhancing resilience and strengthening investor trust.”

data.title: Hong Kong’s CK Hutchison reports 7% gain in underlying profit
data.description:
CK Hutchison Holdings, one of the flagship companies owned by Hong Kong billionaire Li Ka-shing’s family, reported a 7 per cent increase in underlying profit for 2025, as the company predicted its businesses would face “new and perhaps unforeseen challenges” in 2026.
The ports-to-telecoms conglomerate said on Thursday that underlying profit reached HK$22.3 billion (US$2.85 ‌billion) last year, compared with HK$20.8 billion a year earlier.
Including a one-time ⁠accounting loss, net profit fell ⁠31 per cent from a year earlier to HK$11.84 ‌billion, it said.
The one-time non-cash loss of HK$10.92 billion was related to the UK telecoms merger of 3UK with Vodafone. CK Hutchison received about £1.3 billion (US$1.73 billion) in net cash from the merger, according to its filing with the Hong Kong stock exchange.
“Geopolitical pressure has led to a meaningful legal conflict with the Panamanian state relating to the group’s container terminal operations there,” said chairman Victor Li Tzar-kuoi. “Notwithstanding this backdrop, the group’s highly diversified business and geographic spread largely mitigates the impact of adverse developments in any particular sector or country. Strong cash generation in the year has placed the group in a solid financial position.”
He added that the group would continue to “look for opportunities to enhance value for our shareholders through major transaction activity”.
CK Hutchison’s net debt to net total capital ratio stood at 13.9 per cent at the end of 2025, he said.
“Without doubt, the group’s businesses will face new and perhaps unforeseen challenges in 2026,” Li said. “However, as usual, the group will maintain its disciplined capital allocation, cash flow and liability management, as well as its strong financial profile to ensure it continues to deliver a stable performance.”
In a results briefing, Li said the group faced “absolutely no pressure” to sell any assets, but would consider divestments where value could be realised.
With an energy crunch gripping the global economy owing to the Iran conflict, Li noted that the company’s diversified global operations had allowed it to offset pressures, with its investment in Canada’s Cenovus Energy benefiting from rising oil prices.
Meanwhile, profit at CK Asset Holdings, the group’s other flagship company, fell more than a fifth to HK$10.84 billion, dragged by a negative investment property revaluation, it said in a separate filing.
Without the revaluation, its underlying profit rose 2.7 per cent to HK$11.96 billion, it said.
“The group is well-positioned to meet the challenges of an increasingly volatile operating environment with its well-diversified and resilient portfolio of high-quality investments and assets that span across various geographies and sectors,” Li said.
The developer said it expected a busy year ahead, with Hong Kong’s residential market sustaining a recovery that began in the second quarter of 2025, according to official data.
New launches would include projects on Borrett Road in the Mid-Levels and Kai Tak in East Kowloon, alongside potential projects in To Kwa Wan, the company said.
Meanwhile, infrastructure and energy company CK Infrastructure reported that its 2025 profit increased 2 per cent to HK$8.26 billion.
It expected to complete the £10.5 billion sale of UK Power Networks to French multinational electric utility Engie, which was announced last month, in the first half of the year.

data.title: Alibaba and Baidu lift cloud prices by up to 34% amid AI demand surgeThe Chinese firms’ price increases are ‘a reasonable response to evolving market conditions’, says IDC China analyst Cui Tingting.
data.description:
The cloud-computing units of Alibaba Group Holding and Baidu are raising prices for certain services by as much as 34 per cent, following similar moves by their American peers earlier this year as artificial intelligence demand and infrastructure costs rise.
Starting April 18, Alibaba Cloud’s services running on its AI chips – such as the T-Head Zhengwu 810E unveiled in late January – would cost between 5 and 34 per cent more, the company said in a statement on Wednesday.
The price of its Cloud Parallel File Storage service will rise by 30 per cent. Alibaba owns the South China Morning Post.
Meanwhile, Baidu Cloud said on Wednesday that it would increase the price of its AI computing power related services by around 5 to 30 per cent, and its parallel file storage system by 30 per cent. The changes will also take effect on April 18.

Cui Tingting, a research manager at IDC China, described the price increases as “a reasonable response to evolving market conditions”.
She said that demand for computing power had surged amid an intensifying global AI race, while many major AI infrastructure players had announced plans to increase strategic investment in the field over the next three to five years.
“With a massive future supply gap [anticipated], hardware suppliers are accelerating price rises in response to shifting supply-demand forecasts, which in turn exacerbates the upward trend in supply chain costs, driving up costs in the cloud-computing market,” Cui said.
Major US cloud service providers raised their prices earlier this year.
Google announced increases of up to 100 per cent for certain services – including CDN Interconnect and Direct Peering for North American users – from May 1, while Amazon Web Services raised rates for its EC2 Capacity Blocks for ML service by about 15 per cent globally.
Cui said that further price increases could not be ruled out if supply chain costs continued to climb. Cloud providers were also factoring in mandatory future expenditure on research and development and market operations, she added.
“It is worth noting that the recent escalation of tensions in the Middle East has, to some extent, affected the supply of energy, chemicals and metal raw materials essential for hardware production, which may further exacerbate global hardware supply shortages,” Cui said.
Tencent Cloud last week ended its limited-time free public beta for the GLM 5, MiniMax 2.5 and Kimi 2.5 models.
It also increased prices for its Hunyuan series – the HY2.0 Instruct and HY2.0 Think models – raising token costs more than fivefold.
Tencent on Wednesday did not immediately respond to a request for comment on whether the price increase would extend to other relevant services.
Alibaba Cloud led China’s AI cloud services sector in the first half of 2025 with a 36 per cent market share. ByteDance’s Volcano Engine, Huawei Cloud and Tencent Cloud followed with 15, 13 and 7 per cent, respectively, according to market intelligence provider Omdia.

data.title: China presses EV makers to end price war and focus on innovation as demand cools
data.description:
Chinese authorities have renewed their push for stricter oversight of the electric vehicle (EV) market, urging carmakers to move away from aggressive discounting and focus instead on technological innovation.
The directive comes as domestic manufacturers struggle with cooling demand and the phasing out of long-standing government subsidies.
At a high-level meeting on March 17, the Ministry of Industry and Information Technology, the National Development and Reform Commission and the State Administration for Market Regulation, gathered 17 major carmakers to “further regulate the competition order”, in the latest attempt to stabilise a sector defined by a “sticky” and often destructive price war.
Officials vowed to strengthen price monitoring and cost investigations, while urging companies to strictly honour their 60-day payment cycle commitment to suppliers.
According to a February 2026 progress report by the China Association of Automobile Manufacturers, a government-backed industry consortium, the 17 carmakers reduced average payment cycles to 54 days, with four settling in under 50 days.

The extended payment cycle has long been used to cut costs and stay competitive in China’s vast automobile market, but it has squeezed supply-chain profitability even as Beijing has sought to counter deflationary pressures.
The government urged companies to pivot from price-based competition to innovation-led growth, accelerating the development of domestic automotive chips and foundational software to reduce reliance on foreign technology, and speeding up breakthroughs in self-driving systems.
With direct subsidies gone, Beijing has shifted focus to organic demand and global expansion through “trade-in” programmes and the wider application of new-energy heavy trucks.
The vehicle trade-in subsidy scheme was renewed at the end of 2025, offering cash incentives for buyers replacing older vehicles, with higher subsidies for new-energy models.
Authorities also planned policy documents to foster a healthy “car modification” market to unlock untapped consumer potential. To support these efforts, the government aims to enhance financial credit services and international logistics, ensuring smoother pathways for Chinese EV exports and overseas operations.
In the first two months of the year, China sold more than 1.7 million new-energy vehicles, down 7 per cent from a year earlier, as consumers became more budget-conscious after the phasing out of the 10 per cent sales tax exemption.
EV makers including BYD shifted their selling points to supercharging technology and updated intelligent driving systems in recent launches, in addition to speeding up overseas sales.
Sales of EVs in Europe jumped 33 per cent year on year to 4.3 million units in 2025, according to data from London-based minerals research and pricing firm Benchmark Mineral Intelligence.
